sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 2

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 2:1
items.html:
Explaining the thought of creation.
Now we come to the explanation of the form of the thought of creation. It is certain that "the end of the act is in the preliminary thought." Even in a corporeal person, of many thoughts, the end of the act is present in his thought at the start. For example, when one is engaged in building a house, we understand that the first thought he had in the matter was the form of a house to dwell in; hence a multiplicity of thoughts and a multiplicity of actions precede, until he completes that form which he had conceived at the outset — and that form comes to him at the end of all his actions. Thus you see that the end of the act is in the preliminary thought. Now the end of the act — the pivot and the purpose for whose sake all these were created — is to delight His creatures, as it is written in the Zohar. And it is known that His thought is completed and acts at once, for He is not a man, bound to instruments of action; rather, the thought alone completes the whole act instantly and at once. Accordingly it is understood that as soon as He thought of the creation — to delight His creatures — this Light at once extended and expanded from Him, in the full character and stature and full height of the pleasures He conceived in it, all of which is included in that thought which we call "the thought of creation." Understand this well, for this is a place where they said to be brief. And know that this thought of creation is what we call "the Light of Ein Sof," for of His very Essence we have no utterance or word by which to define Him by any name whatsoever. Remember this.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 2:2
items.html:
From the force of the desire to bestow in the Emanator, the desire to receive is necessarily born in the emanated. It is the vessel in which the emanated receives its abundance.
This is what the Rav said — that at first the Light of Ein Sof filled the whole of reality. Meaning: since the Creator thought to delight the created beings, and the Light expanded from Him and came forth from before Him, as it were, the desire to receive His pleasure was at once imprinted in this Light. And judge also that this desire is the full measure of the greatness of the expanding Light — that is, the measure of His Light and abundance is exactly the measure in which He wishes to give delight, no less and no more. Study this closely. Therefore we call the essence of this desire to receive, imprinted in this Light by the force of His thought, by the name "place." For example, when we say that one person has place to receive a meal of a pound of bread while another cannot eat more than half a pound of bread — of what place are we speaking? Not of the size of the intestines, but of the size of the appetite and the desire to eat. You see, then, that the measure of the place for receiving the bread depends on the measure of the desire and craving for eating. All the more so in spirituality, where the desire to receive the abundance is the place of the abundance, and the abundance is measured by the measure of the desire.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 2:3
items.html:
The desire to receive included in the thought of creation brought it out of His Essence into the discernment named Ein Sof.
By this we are relieved and can perceive how the Light of Ein Sof departed from His Essence — of which we have no utterance or word — so as to be defined by the name "the Light of Ein Sof," as above. It is by reason of the above discernment: in this Light is included the desire to receive from His Essence, and this is a new form, which is not included, God forbid, in His Essence at all — for from whom would He receive? And this form is also the entire greatness of this Light, as above. Study this closely, for it is impossible to elaborate here.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 2:4
items.html:
Before the restriction, the disparity of form of the desire to receive was not discernible.
Yet for all its power, this new form was not discerned as a disparity from His Light. This is the secret of what is written in Pirkei de-Rabbi Eliezer, that before the world was created, He was One and His Name One: "He" indicates the Light in Ein Sof, and "His Name" indicates the place — the secret of the desire to receive from His Essence that is included in the Light of Ein Sof. It teaches us that He and His Name are One: His Name — the secret of Malchut of Ein Sof, the secret of the desire, namely the desire to receive imprinted in the whole of reality that was included in the thought of creation before the restriction — was discerned with no disparity of form or difference from the Light in it. The Light and the place are literally one. For if there had been in the place any change or diminution with respect to the Light of Ein Sof, there would certainly have been two discernments there, as above. Study this closely.

items.sefariaRef: Talmud Eser HaSefirot, Section I, Histaklut Penimit 2:5
items.html:
Restriction means that Malchut of Ein Sof diminished the desire to receive in her; then the Light departed, for there is no Light without a vessel.
This is the matter of the restriction: the desire to receive included in the Light of Ein Sof — called Malchut of Ein Sof, the secret of the thought of creation in Ein Sof, as above, which comprises the whole of reality — adorned herself to ascend and to equalize her form more fully with His Essence. Therefore she diminished her desire to receive His abundance in the fourth phase of the desire, as will be written below, with the intention that thereby the worlds would be emanated and created, down to this world — so that in this way the form of the desire to receive would be corrected and return to the form of bestowal, and thereby come into equivalence of form with the Emanator. And once she had diminished the desire to receive, the Light naturally departed from there — for it is already known that the Light depends on the desire, and the desire is the place of the Light, and there is no coercion in spirituality.
